license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Engine Lifecycle State Changed: Started
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell engine session starts. The HostApplication field is the most valuable field — it records the full command line used to invoke PowerShell, including any encoded arguments. Suspicious values include base64-encoded -EncodedCommand strings, unusual host processes (not powershell.exe or powershell_ise.exe), or invocation from scripting engines. Correlate with 403 to track session duration.

investigationPivots: HostApplication is the primary pivot — flag -EncodedCommand, -WindowStyle Hidden, -NonInteractive, or -ExecutionPolicy Bypass in the invocation string
HostApplication values showing invocation from wscript.exe, mshta.exe, cmd.exe, or Office processes are high-priority
Correlate with EID 403 to calculate session duration — very short sessions may indicate one-liner or fileless execution
commonFalsePositives: Scheduled tasks and software agents invoking PowerShell for automation

entries.title: Engine Lifecycle State Changed: Stopped
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell engine session ends. Pair with event 400 to calculate session duration. Abnormally short sessions may indicate one-liner or fileless execution. Absence of a matching 403 after 400 may indicate a crash or forceful termination.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 400 to calculate session duration — sub-second sessions are characteristic of one-liner payload execution
Missing EID 403 after EID 400 may indicate the engine was forcibly killed (e.g., by a security tool or crash)

entries.title: Command Started
entries.details: Generated at the start of each command execution in the classic Windows PowerShell log. Records the command name in the CommandLine field. High volume in normal operation — most useful when correlated with 4103 or 4104 for context on specific command invocations. Pairs with 501 to track command duration.

investigationPivots: CommandLine field records the command name — use as a coarse filter to identify suspicious cmdlets, then correlate with EID 4103 for full parameter detail
Pair with EID 501 to calculate per-command execution time
commonFalsePositives: High-volume event during any PowerShell session — filter to specific command names of interest

entries.title: Command Stopped
entries.details: Generated when a command execution ends in the classic Windows PowerShell log. Pairs with 500 to track command lifecycle. On its own, limited investigative value — use in conjunction with 4103 or 4104 to reconstruct full execution context.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 500 to calculate per-command duration
On its own this event has low fidelity — value increases when correlated with EID 4103 or 4104
commonFalsePositives: Any normal command completion during a PowerShell session

entries.title: Provider Lifecycle State Changed: Started
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell provider is loaded into the session. The ProviderName field identifies the provider. Monitor for WSMan provider (indicates PowerShell remoting), Registry provider (registry manipulation), Certificate provider (certificate store access), and FileSystem provider. Unexpected providers in non-interactive sessions may indicate attacker tradecraft.

investigationPivots: WSMan provider loaded in a non-interactive or automated session may indicate PowerShell remoting activity
Registry and Certificate providers loaded in unexpected contexts warrant review of subsequent EID 4103/4104 for what was accessed
Correlate provider load order with EID 400 HostApplication to understand the session context
commonFalsePositives: All standard providers (FileSystem, Registry, Certificate, etc.) load during normal interactive PowerShell sessions

entries.title: Pipeline Execution Details
entries.details: Generated in the classic Windows PowerShell log with pipeline execution details including command name and pipeline ID. Lower fidelity than the operational log event 4103 — does not capture full parameters or script content. Present on older systems where the operational log or module logging is not configured. Superseded by 4103 on modern, properly configured systems.

investigationPivots: Primarily useful on systems without EID 4103 module logging configured — treat as a fallback data source
Command name in this event can serve as a coarse filter; investigate further using other available telemetry
commonFalsePositives: Any pipeline execution in a PowerShell session

entries.title: PowerShell Error
entries.details: Generated when an error occurs during pipeline execution in the operational log. Error messages in the ErrorRecord field may reveal attack artifacts — failed execution attempts, blocked cmdlets, missing modules, or access denied errors. Clusters of 4100 events targeting specific commands (e.g., Invoke-Mimikatz, Add-MpPreference) can indicate active attack or defense evasion failure. Correlate with 4104 for the script that triggered the error.

investigationPivots: ErrorRecord field may expose command names, module names, or file paths associated with attacker tooling even when execution failed
Clusters of errors referencing known offensive cmdlets (e.g., Invoke-Mimikatz, Set-MpPreference, Add-MpPreference) are high-confidence attack indicators
Correlate with EID 4104 on the same ScriptBlockId to review the full script that generated the error
commonFalsePositives: Script errors in legitimate automation due to missing modules or version mismatches
Interactive users running commands with incorrect syntax

entries.title: Module Logging
entries.summary: Module logging captured full pipeline execution with parameters.
entries.details: Generated when module logging is enabled via GPO (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows PowerShell > Turn on Module Logging). Records the full command with all parameter values, pipeline input/output, and the user context. Higher fidelity than classic log event 800. Required GPO setting per module or wildcard (*). Correlate with 4104 for script block content.

investigationPivots: Full parameter values are recorded — look for credential strings, encoded payloads, or remote host names in parameter fields
SubjectUserName and HostApplication establish user and process context
Correlate with EID 4104 to link module-level execution to the script block that invoked it
commonFalsePositives: High-volume event in environments with module logging enabled — filter to specific modules or cmdlets of interest
Legitimate administrative scripts generating large volumes of module logging events

keyFields.name: Payload
keyFields.description: The pipeline execution detail or command output; search for download cradles, encoded strings, and suspicious cmdlets

keyFields.name: ContextInfo
keyFields.description: Contextual information including host application, runspace, and connected user

keyFields.name: HostName
keyFields.description: PowerShell host running the command (e.g., ConsoleHost, Windows PowerShell ISE); non-standard hosts are suspicious

keyFields.name: HostApplication
keyFields.description: Full path of the application hosting PowerShell; unexpected parents like wscript.exe or mshta.exe are red flags

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging — Suspicious Download Cradle or Obfuscation
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell"
| where EventID == 4103
| where RenderedDescription has_any (
    "IEX", "Invoke-Expression", "DownloadString", "DownloadFile",
    "WebClient", "Net.WebClient", "System.Net.WebClient",
    "FromBase64String", "[Convert]::", "EncodedCommand",
    "Invoke-Mimikatz", "Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Module logging captures all pipeline execution. Payload field contains the command. High volume — tune with additional context filters like HostApplication.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4103
(Payload="*IEX*" OR Payload="*Invoke-Expression*" OR Payload="*DownloadString*"
OR Payload="*WebClient*" OR Payload="*FromBase64String*" OR Payload="*Invoke-Mimikatz*")
| table _time, Computer, User, Payload, ContextInfo
detectionRules.notes: Search the Payload field for known attacker keywords and download cradles.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Activity
detectionRules.rule:
title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Activity
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: powershell
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4103
        Payload|contains:
            - 'IEX'
            - 'Invoke-Expression'
            - 'DownloadString'
            - 'Net.WebClient'
            - 'FromBase64String'
            - 'Invoke-Mimikatz'
            - 'Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection'
falsepositives:
    - Legitimate administration scripts
    - Software deployment tools
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Baseline keyword list; expand with environment-specific attacker TTPs.

entries.title: Script Block Logging
entries.details: The highest-value PowerShell event. Generated when script block logging is enabled (GPO: Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging). Captures the full text of every compiled script block after deobfuscation — meaning even obfuscated or encoded scripts appear in cleartext here. Level 3 (Warning) is generated automatically without any GPO requirement when AMSI flags a script block as suspicious. Large scripts are split across multiple sequential events sharing a MessageNumber/MessageTotal field. Pivot on ScriptBlockText for IOC matching, SubjectUserName, and HostApplication.

prerequisites.description: Script Block Logging must be enabled via Group Policy ('Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging'). Exception: Level 3 (Warning) events for AMSI-flagged suspicious scripts are generated automatically without GPO configuration.

investigationPivots: ScriptBlockText is post-deobfuscation — even heavily encoded or concatenated scripts appear in cleartext here
Warning-level (3) events are AMSI-triggered and generated regardless of GPO configuration — always treat these as high priority
MessageNumber/MessageTotal fields identify fragments of a split script — reassemble in order for full content
Search ScriptBlockText for known IOCs: download cradles (Net.WebClient, IEX, Invoke-Expression), credential access (mimikatz, sekurlsa), and lateral movement cmdlets
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate security tools and scripts that use encoding or obfuscation for intellectual property protection
AMSI-triggered Warning events from security testing or red team activity in authorized environments

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockText
keyFields.description: Full text of the script block being executed; the primary field for detecting malicious PowerShell content

keyFields.name: Path
keyFields.description: Path of the script file if executed from disk; empty means executed interactively or from memory

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockId
keyFields.description: GUID uniquely identifying the script block; large scripts are split across multiple events with the same ID

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block — Attacker Toolkit Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell"
| where EventID == 4104
| where RenderedDescription has_any (
    "Invoke-Mimikatz", "Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection", "Invoke-Shellcode",
    "PowerSploit", "Get-GPPPassword", "Invoke-CredentialInjection",
    "AmsiUtils", "amsiInitFailed", "System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils",
    "-EncodedCommand", "-enc ", "[System.Convert]::FromBase64String",
    "VirtualAlloc", "WriteProcessMemory", "CreateThread"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Script block logging captures full script content even for obfuscated scripts (AMSI sees decoded content). Higher fidelity than 4103 for detecting known tools.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging — Malicious Content
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4104
(ScriptBlock_Text="*Invoke-Mimikatz*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*AmsiUtils*"
OR ScriptBlock_Text="*amsiInitFailed*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*FromBase64String*"
OR ScriptBlock_Text="*VirtualAlloc*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection*")
| table _time, Computer, User, ScriptBlock_Text, Path
detectionRules.notes: The ScriptBlock_Text field contains the full decoded script content.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging Malicious Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
title: PowerShell Script Block Logging Malicious Keywords
status: stable
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: powershell
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4104
        ScriptBlockText|contains:
            - 'Invoke-Mimikatz'
            - 'AmsiUtils'
            - 'amsiInitFailed'
            - 'Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection'
            - 'PowerSploit'
            - 'Get-GPPPassword'
            - 'VirtualAlloc'
            - 'WriteProcessMemory'
falsepositives:
    - Security tooling and EDR agents
    - Penetration testing
level: high
detectionRules.notes: High confidence rule; these strings rarely appear in legitimate scripts.

entries.title: Started Invocation
entries.details: Generated at the start of a script block invocation. Pairs with 4106 to bracket execution time. The ScriptBlockId field links back to the 4104 event that captured the script content. Useful for correlating execution timing with other log sources (e.g., network, process creation).

investigationPivots: ScriptBlockId links this event back to the EID 4104 script block content — use it to identify what was being executed
Correlate timestamp with network connections and Security EID 4688 process creation to build a full execution timeline

entries.title: Completed Invocation
entries.details: Generated when a script block invocation ends. Pairs with 4105 to track execution duration. The ScriptBlockId field links back to the 4104 event that captured the script content. Absence of a matching 4106 after 4105 may indicate a crash, forced termination, or runspace hijack.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 4105 using ScriptBlockId to calculate execution duration
Missing EID 4106 after EID 4105 may indicate the runspace was killed, crashed, or hijacked — correlate with Application EID 1000
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate script block completing normally
